instruction: Design a minimal async runtime in Rust that demonstrates how executors, wakers, and futures work together. Show the core event loop, task scheduling, and how IO readiness notifications drive future polling.
output:
<think>
Rust's async/await is built on three pillars: Future (a value that may not be ready yet), Waker (a callback to notify the executor), and Executor (polls futures when woken). Understanding how these compose is essential for systems programming.

The Future trait has one method: poll(cx: &mut Context) -> Poll<Output>. It returns Ready(value) or Pending. When Pending, the future must arrange for the Waker (from the Context) to be called when progress can be made.

The executor's job: maintain a queue of tasks, poll each task, and when a task returns Pending, wait until its waker is called before polling again. The waker puts the task back on the ready queue.

My minimal runtime:
1. Task: wraps a pinned, boxed future + a waker
2. Executor: owns a VecDeque of ready tasks, runs them in a loop
3. Waker: when called, pushes the task back onto the ready queue
4. Timer future: demonstrates async without IO (uses a background thread)

The waker mechanism is the trickiest part. I need to implement RawWaker/RawWakerVTable to create a custom waker that knows how to reschedule a task. In production runtimes like tokio, this is backed by an epoll/kqueue/io_uring event loop.

I'll keep it simple: use a channel for the ready queue, and a thread-based timer for demonstrating async wakeup.
</think>

```rust
use std::collections::VecDeque;
use std::future::Future;
use std::pin::Pin;
use std::sync::{Arc, Mutex};
use std::task::{Context, Poll, RawWaker, RawWakerVTable, Waker};
use std::thread;
use std::time::{Duration, Instant};

// === Task: a boxed future + ability to reschedule ===

type BoxFuture = Pin<Box<dyn Future<Output = ()> + Send>>;

struct Task {
    future: Mutex<BoxFuture>,
    queue: Arc<Mutex<VecDeque<Arc<Task>>>>,
}

impl Task {
    fn new(future: BoxFuture, queue: Arc<Mutex<VecDeque<Arc<Task>>>>) -> Arc<Self> {
        Arc::new(Task {
            future: Mutex::new(future),
            queue,
        })
    }
}

// === Waker implementation ===
// The waker's job: put the task back on the ready queue when called.

fn task_waker(task: Arc<Task>) -> Waker {
    let raw = Arc::into_raw(task) as *const ();
    let vtable = &VTABLE;
    unsafe { Waker::from_raw(RawWaker::new(raw, vtable)) }
}

const VTABLE: RawWakerVTable = RawWakerVTable::new(
    |ptr| {
        // clone: increment Arc refcount
        let arc = unsafe { Arc::from_raw(ptr as *const Task) };
        let cloned = arc.clone();
        std::mem::forget(arc); // Don't drop the original
        RawWaker::new(Arc::into_raw(cloned) as *const (), &VTABLE)
    },
    |ptr| {
        // wake: reschedule task, consume the Arc
        let arc = unsafe { Arc::from_raw(ptr as *const Task) };
        arc.queue.lock().unwrap().push_back(arc.clone());
    },
    |ptr| {
        // wake_by_ref: reschedule without consuming
        let arc = unsafe { Arc::from_raw(ptr as *const Task) };
        arc.queue.lock().unwrap().push_back(arc.clone());
        std::mem::forget(arc);
    },
    |ptr| {
        // drop: decrement Arc refcount
        unsafe { Arc::from_raw(ptr as *const Task) };
    },
);

// === Executor: the event loop ===

struct MiniExecutor {
    queue: Arc<Mutex<VecDeque<Arc<Task>>>>,
}

impl MiniExecutor {
    fn new() -> Self {
        MiniExecutor {
            queue: Arc::new(Mutex::new(VecDeque::new())),
        }
    }

    fn spawn(&self, future: impl Future<Output = ()> + Send + 'static) {
        let task = Task::new(Box::pin(future), self.queue.clone());
        self.queue.lock().unwrap().push_back(task);
    }

    fn run(&self) {
        loop {
            let task = {
                let mut q = self.queue.lock().unwrap();
                q.pop_front()
            };

            match task {
                Some(task) => {
                    let waker = task_waker(task.clone());
                    let mut cx = Context::from_waker(&waker);
                    let mut future = task.future.lock().unwrap();

                    match future.as_mut().poll(&mut cx) {
                        Poll::Ready(()) => {
                            // Task complete, don't reschedule
                        }
                        Poll::Pending => {
                            // Future will call waker when ready
                        }
                    }
                }
                None => {
                    // No tasks ready — in production, we'd block on epoll/kqueue
                    thread::sleep(Duration::from_millis(1));
                    
                    // Exit if truly nothing left
                    if self.queue.lock().unwrap().is_empty() {
                        break;
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }
}

// === Timer Future: demonstrates async wakeup ===

struct TimerFuture {
    deadline: Instant,
    started: bool,
}

impl TimerFuture {
    fn new(duration: Duration) -> Self {
        TimerFuture {
            deadline: Instant::now() + duration,
            started: false,
        }
    }
}

impl Future for TimerFuture {
    type Output = ();

    fn poll(mut self: Pin<&mut Self>, cx: &mut Context<'_>) -> Poll<()> {
        if Instant::now() >= self.deadline {
            return Poll::Ready(());
        }

        if !self.started {
            self.started = true;
            let waker = cx.waker().clone();
            let deadline = self.deadline;

            // Spawn a thread to wake us when the timer expires
            thread::spawn(move || {
                let remaining = deadline - Instant::now();
                thread::sleep(remaining);
                waker.wake(); // This puts our task back on the ready queue
            });
        }

        Poll::Pending
    }
}

// Convenience function
async fn sleep(duration: Duration) {
    TimerFuture::new(duration).await;
}

// === Demo ===

fn main() {
    let executor = MiniExecutor::new();

    executor.spawn(async {
        println!("[Task 1] Starting");
        sleep(Duration::from_millis(200)).await;
        println!("[Task 1] After 200ms");
        sleep(Duration::from_millis(100)).await;
        println!("[Task 1] Done");
    });

    executor.spawn(async {
        println!("[Task 2] Starting");
        sleep(Duration::from_millis(100)).await;
        println!("[Task 2] After 100ms");
        sleep(Duration::from_millis(300)).await;
        println!("[Task 2] Done");
    });

    println!("Running mini async runtime...");
    executor.run();
    println!("All tasks completed!");
}
```

instruction: Explain io_uring and implement a reasoning exercise about when to use io_uring vs epoll vs traditional blocking I/O in Rust systems. Include a conceptual implementation of an io_uring-based file reader.
output:
<think>
io_uring is Linux's newest async I/O interface (kernel 5.1+). Unlike epoll (which only does readiness notification — tells you WHEN to read/write) or AIO (which is limited and buggy), io_uring does true asynchronous I/O: you submit operations and get completions without system calls per operation.

Architecture: two ring buffers shared between user space and kernel — the submission queue (SQ) and completion queue (CQ). User pushes submission queue entries (SQEs) to SQ, calls io_uring_enter() (or the kernel polls SQ automatically), and reads completion queue entries (CQEs) from CQ.

Key advantages:
1. Batched syscalls: submit many operations with one syscall (or zero with SQPOLL mode)
2. True async: file I/O, network I/O, and other ops all unified
3. Zero-copy: registered buffers avoid kernel-user copies
4. Linked operations: chain dependent ops without round-trips

When to use what:
- Blocking I/O: simple tools, few connections, when latency doesn't matter
- epoll: network servers with many connections (the Go/Nginx model). Readiness-based, works with non-blocking sockets.
- io_uring: high-performance file I/O, or when you need both file and network I/O unified. Also great for reducing syscall overhead at very high throughput.

io_uring vs epoll for networking: epoll requires the application to do the actual read/write (readiness notification). io_uring does the I/O in the kernel (completion notification). For most network servers, epoll is sufficient. io_uring shines when you need high-throughput file I/O or want to minimize syscall count.

I'll implement a conceptual io_uring file reader in Rust using the io-uring crate patterns (without the actual crate dependency, showing the structure).
</think>

```rust
//! Conceptual io_uring-based file reader in Rust.
//! This demonstrates the pattern; real code would use the `io-uring` crate.

use std::collections::HashMap;
use std::fs::File;
use std::io::{self, Read};
use std::os::unix::io::AsRawFd;

/// Represents an io_uring submission queue entry.
#[derive(Debug)]
struct SubmissionEntry {
    opcode: Opcode,
    fd: i32,
    offset: u64,
    buffer: Vec<u8>,
    user_data: u64,  // identifier to match with completion
}

/// Represents an io_uring completion queue entry.
#[derive(Debug)]
struct CompletionEntry {
    user_data: u64,
    result: i32,     // bytes read or error
    buffer: Vec<u8>, // returned buffer with data
}

#[derive(Debug)]
enum Opcode {
    Read,
    Write,
    Nop,
}

/// Conceptual io_uring instance.
/// In reality, this would be backed by mmap'd ring buffers shared with the kernel.
struct IoUring {
    submissions: Vec<SubmissionEntry>,
    next_id: u64,
}

impl IoUring {
    fn new(_queue_depth: u32) -> io::Result<Self> {
        // Real: io_uring_setup() syscall to create ring buffers
        Ok(IoUring {
            submissions: Vec::new(),
            next_id: 0,
        })
    }

    /// Queue a read operation (doesn't execute yet).
    fn prepare_read(&mut self, fd: i32, offset: u64, len: usize) -> u64 {
        let id = self.next_id;
        self.next_id += 1;

        self.submissions.push(SubmissionEntry {
            opcode: Opcode::Read,
            fd,
            offset,
            buffer: vec![0u8; len],
            user_data: id,
        });

        id
    }

    /// Submit all queued operations and wait for completions.
    /// Real: single io_uring_enter() syscall submits ALL pending ops.
    fn submit_and_wait(&mut self) -> io::Result<Vec<CompletionEntry>> {
        let mut completions = Vec::new();

        // Simulate: in reality, the kernel processes these asynchronously
        for entry in self.submissions.drain(..) {
            match entry.opcode {
                Opcode::Read => {
                    // Simulate pread at offset
                    let mut buffer = entry.buffer;
                    // In reality: kernel does this asynchronously
                    // Here we simulate with synchronous read for demo
                    use std::os::unix::io::FromRawFd;
                    let mut file = unsafe { File::from_raw_fd(entry.fd) };
                    use std::io::Seek;
                    file.seek(std::io::SeekFrom::Start(entry.offset))?;
                    let bytes_read = file.read(&mut buffer)?;
                    std::mem::forget(file); // Don't close the fd

                    completions.push(CompletionEntry {
                        user_data: entry.user_data,
                        result: bytes_read as i32,
                        buffer,
                    });
                }
                _ => {}
            }
        }

        Ok(completions)
    }
}

/// Decision framework: when to use each I/O model.
fn io_model_decision(scenario: &str) -> &'static str {
    match scenario {
        "simple_cli_tool" =>
            "Blocking I/O: simple, correct, no concurrency needed",

        "web_server_10k_connections" =>
            "epoll: readiness-based is perfect for many network connections. \
             Non-blocking sockets + event loop (tokio/mio pattern)",

        "database_engine_random_reads" =>
            "io_uring: true async file I/O eliminates thread pool for disk reads. \
             Batched submissions reduce syscall overhead. \
             Kernel handles scheduling across NVMe queues",

        "log_processing_sequential" =>
            "Blocking I/O with large buffers: sequential access is cache-friendly. \
             mmap is also good here. io_uring adds complexity for minimal gain",

        "proxy_server_high_throughput" =>
            "io_uring: unified network + splice operations. \
             Zero-copy forwarding with IORING_OP_SPLICE. \
             Fewer syscalls than epoll+read+write per request",

        "embedded_system_limited_kernel" =>
            "epoll or poll: io_uring requires kernel 5.1+. \
             Older kernels need epoll. Very old systems may need select/poll",

        _ => "Depends on workload characteristics"
    }
}

fn main() {
    // Demonstrate the decision framework
    let scenarios = [
        "simple_cli_tool",
        "web_server_10k_connections",
        "database_engine_random_reads",
        "log_processing_sequential",
        "proxy_server_high_throughput",
        "embedded_system_limited_kernel",
    ];

    println!("=== I/O Model Decision Framework ===\n");
    for scenario in &scenarios {
        println!("Scenario: {}", scenario);
        println!("  -> {}\n", io_model_decision(scenario));
    }

    // Conceptual io_uring usage
    println!("=== Conceptual io_uring File Reader ===");
    println!("(Shows the API pattern — real implementation uses io-uring crate)\n");

    // Create a test file
    std::fs::write("/tmp/uring_test.txt", "Hello from io_uring! This is async I/O.").unwrap();

    let file = File::open("/tmp/uring_test.txt").unwrap();
    let fd = file.as_raw_fd();

    let mut ring = IoUring::new(64).unwrap();

    // Queue multiple reads (batched — single syscall in real io_uring)
    let id1 = ring.prepare_read(fd, 0, 20);   // Read first 20 bytes
    let id2 = ring.prepare_read(fd, 20, 20);  // Read next 20 bytes

    // Submit all at once and get completions
    let completions = ring.submit_and_wait().unwrap();

    for cqe in &completions {
        let text = String::from_utf8_lossy(&cqe.buffer[..cqe.result as usize]);
        println!("Completion {}: {} bytes -> \"{}\"", cqe.user_data, cqe.result, text);
    }

    std::fs::remove_file("/tmp/uring_test.txt").ok();
    println!("\nAll io_uring demos complete");
}
```

instruction: Implement zero-copy networking concepts in Rust. Show how to avoid unnecessary memory copies when forwarding data between sockets, using techniques like splice, sendfile, and buffer pooling.
output:
<think>
Zero-copy networking minimizes data copies between kernel and user space, crucial for high-throughput proxies, CDNs, and file servers. Without zero-copy, data follows: kernel buffer -> user buffer -> kernel buffer (2 copies + 2 context switches per operation). With zero-copy, data stays in kernel space.

Key techniques:
1. sendfile(): copy file -> socket directly in kernel (1 copy or 0 with DMA)
2. splice(): move data between two file descriptors via a pipe (kernel-only transfer)
3. MSG_ZEROCOPY: send socket data without copying from user space
4. Buffer pooling: reuse allocated buffers instead of allocating per-request
5. writev/readv: scatter-gather I/O to avoid concatenation copies

sendfile is for file->socket transfers (serving static files). splice is more general (socket->socket via pipe, great for proxies). Buffer pooling reduces allocation overhead for the cases where data must pass through user space.

In Rust, these are accessed through nix crate or raw libc calls. I'll implement:
1. A file server using sendfile (zero-copy file serving)
2. A conceptual proxy using splice (zero-copy forwarding)
3. A buffer pool for non-zero-copy paths
4. Scatter-gather I/O with IoSlice

The key insight for proxy workloads: with splice, data goes from the client socket to a pipe to the backend socket, never touching user space. This can double throughput compared to read+write.
</think>

```rust
use std::fs::File;
use std::io::{self, Read, Write, IoSlice};
use std::net::{TcpListener, TcpStream};
use std::os::unix::io::{AsRawFd, RawFd};
use std::sync::Mutex;

// === sendfile: zero-copy file serving ===

/// Serve a file over a TCP connection using sendfile (zero-copy).
/// Data goes directly from the file's page cache to the network buffer.
fn serve_file_zerocopy(socket: &TcpStream, file: &File) -> io::Result<usize> {
    let sock_fd = socket.as_raw_fd();
    let file_fd = file.as_raw_fd();
    let file_size = file.metadata()?.len() as usize;
    
    let mut offset: libc::off_t = 0;
    let mut total_sent = 0;
    
    while total_sent < file_size {
        let remaining = file_size - total_sent;
        // SAFETY: valid fds, offset points to valid memory
        let sent = unsafe {
            libc::sendfile(
                sock_fd,
                file_fd,
                &mut offset,
                remaining,
            )
        };
        
        if sent < 0 {
            return Err(io::Error::last_os_error());
        }
        total_sent += sent as usize;
    }
    
    Ok(total_sent)
}


// === splice: zero-copy socket-to-socket forwarding ===

/// Forward data between two sockets via a pipe (zero-copy proxy).
/// Data flow: source_socket -> pipe -> dest_socket (never enters user space)
fn splice_forward(src: RawFd, dst: RawFd, len: usize) -> io::Result<usize> {
    // Create a pipe as the intermediary
    let mut pipe_fds = [0i32; 2];
    if unsafe { libc::pipe(pipe_fds.as_mut_ptr()) } < 0 {
        return Err(io::Error::last_os_error());
    }
    let (pipe_read, pipe_write) = (pipe_fds[0], pipe_fds[1]);
    
    let flags = libc::SPLICE_F_MOVE | libc::SPLICE_F_NONBLOCK;
    let mut total = 0;
    
    while total < len {
        // Move data from source to pipe
        let spliced_in = unsafe {
            libc::splice(
                src,
                std::ptr::null_mut(),
                pipe_write,
                std::ptr::null_mut(),
                len - total,
                flags as u32,
            )
        };
        
        if spliced_in <= 0 {
            break;
        }
        
        // Move data from pipe to destination
        let mut remaining = spliced_in as usize;
        while remaining > 0 {
            let spliced_out = unsafe {
                libc::splice(
                    pipe_read,
                    std::ptr::null_mut(),
                    dst,
                    std::ptr::null_mut(),
                    remaining,
                    flags as u32,
                )
            };
            
            if spliced_out <= 0 {
                break;
            }
            remaining -= spliced_out as usize;
        }
        
        total += spliced_in as usize;
    }
    
    // Close pipe
    unsafe {
        libc::close(pipe_read);
        libc::close(pipe_write);
    }
    
    Ok(total)
}


// === Buffer Pool: reuse buffers for non-zero-copy paths ===

struct BufferPool {
    pool: Mutex<Vec<Vec<u8>>>,
    buffer_size: usize,
}

impl BufferPool {
    fn new(capacity: usize, buffer_size: usize) -> Self {
        let pool = (0..capacity)
            .map(|_| vec![0u8; buffer_size])
            .collect();
        BufferPool {
            pool: Mutex::new(pool),
            buffer_size,
        }
    }
    
    fn acquire(&self) -> Vec<u8> {
        self.pool.lock().unwrap()
            .pop()
            .unwrap_or_else(|| vec![0u8; self.buffer_size])
    }
    
    fn release(&self, mut buf: Vec<u8>) {
        buf.clear();
        buf.resize(self.buffer_size, 0);
        self.pool.lock().unwrap().push(buf);
    }
}


// === Scatter-Gather I/O: avoid concatenation copies ===

fn write_http_response(
    stream: &mut TcpStream,
    headers: &[u8],
    body: &[u8],
) -> io::Result<usize> {
    // writev sends multiple buffers in one syscall without copying them together
    let bufs = [
        IoSlice::new(headers),
        IoSlice::new(b"\r\n"),
        IoSlice::new(body),
    ];
    stream.write_vectored(&bufs)
}


fn main() -> io::Result<()> {
    println!("=== Zero-Copy Networking Techniques ===\n");
    
    // Demo: buffer pool
    let pool = BufferPool::new(16, 8192);
    let buf = pool.acquire();
    println!("Buffer pool: acquired {} byte buffer", buf.len());
    pool.release(buf);
    println!("Buffer pool: released back to pool");
    
    // Demo: scatter-gather write
    println!("\nScatter-gather I/O avoids copying headers + body into one buffer.");
    println!("writev sends [headers, CRLF, body] in a single syscall.");
    
    println!("\n=== Performance comparison ===");
    println!("Traditional proxy (read+write):   2 copies + 2 syscalls per chunk");
    println!("splice proxy (pipe forwarding):   0 copies + 2 splice calls per chunk");
    println!("sendfile (static file serving):   0 copies + 1 syscall");
    println!("writev (multi-buffer response):   0 extra copies + 1 syscall");
    
    println!("\nAll zero-copy demos complete");
    Ok(())
}
```
